input.initialPrompt: Analyze this project for incremental build issues. Identify any custom targets that would unnecessarily re-run on every build even when nothing has changed.

data.reasoningText: The user wants me to analyze the project for incremental build issues. Let me look at the project files to understand what's there.

4.   </PropertyGroup>
5.

9.     </PropertyGroup>

12.

16.     </Exec>
17.     <WriteLinesToFile File="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs"
18.                      Lines="namespace IncrementalBroken { internal static class BuildInfo { public const string GitHash = "$(GitHash)"%3B } }"
19.                      Overwrite="true" />
20.     <ItemGroup>
21.       <Compile Include="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs" />
22.     </ItemGroup>
23.   </Target>
24. </Project>

data.content:
## Incremental Build Issues Found

Both custom targets will **re-run on every build** even when nothing has changed, because they lack `Inputs`/`Outputs` attributes:

### 1. `GenerateTimestamp` (line 6)
- **Problem:** No `Inputs`/`Outputs` declared, so MSBuild can never skip it. Worse, it writes `$([System.DateTime]::Now)` — the output changes every build, which also invalidates downstream targets (like `CoreCompile`), causing a full recompile every time.

### 2. `EmbedGitHash` (line 13)
- **Problem:** No `Inputs`/`Outputs` declared, so it always re-executes. It unconditionally overwrites `git-hash.g.cs` even when the git hash hasn't changed, which triggers recompilation.

---

### How to fix

**`GenerateTimestamp`** — Remove it or gate it behind a property. A volatile timestamp defeats incremental builds by design.

**`EmbedGitHash`** — Add `Inputs`/`Outputs` and only write the file when the hash actually changes:

```xml
<Target Name="EmbedGitHash"
        BeforeTargets="CoreCompile"
        Inputs="$(MSBuildProjectFullPath)"
        Outputs="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs">
  <Exec Command="git rev-parse HEAD" ConsoleToMsBuild="true" IgnoreExitCode="true">
    <Output TaskParameter="ConsoleOutput" PropertyName="GitHash" />
  </Exec>
  <!-- Write to a temp file, then compare before overwriting -->
  <WriteLinesToFile File="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs" ... Overwrite="true"
                   Condition="!Exists('$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs')" />
</Target>
```

A more robust pattern is to write to a `.tmp` file, compare with the existing output, and only copy if different — avoiding timestamp churn on the `.g.cs` file.
